summary: This lesson introduces the past subjunctive (mood tense in the past), explaining how to form it from the 'they' form of the preterite by changing -ron to -ra/-ran. It also introduces 'volver a + infinitive' as a way to say 'again', and shows how 'para que + subjunctive' differs from 'para + infinitive'.

teachings.summary: The past subjunctive is formed from the 'they' form of the preterite (point in the past). Change the final -o of -aron/-ieron to -a to get -aran/-ieran (they). Remove -n for I/he/she. Add -s for informal you. Add -mos (with accent) for we. One form serves both preterite and imperfect contexts.
teachings.rule: They preterite (e.g. hablaron) → change -on to -an = hablaran (they, past subjunctive). Drop -n = hablara (I/he/she). Add -s = hablaras (you informal). Add -mos = habláramos (we).

examples.english: I wanted them to eat with us
I was hoping that he would call me
We needed him to speak with us

examples.spanish: Volví a llamarte para que organizáramos
examples.english: I called you again so that we organized

teachings.notes: The past subjunctive is the same for both line and point contexts. In English you'd say 'would call' but in Spanish you use the past subjunctive instead.

teachings.summary: Volver a + infinitive means 'to do something again'. It uses 'a' because volver is a verb of movement. It's very common in Spanish.
teachings.rule: Volver a + infinitive = to do again. No quiero que vuelva a pasar = I don't want it to happen again.

examples.spanish: No quiero que la puerta vuelva a cerrarse
examples.english: I don't want the door to close again

examples.spanish: No quiero que vuelva a pasar
examples.english: I don't want it to happen again

teachings.notes: An alternative to 'otra vez' for saying 'again'.

teachings.conceptName: Para que + subjunctive vs para + infinitive
teachings.summary: Para + infinitive (in order to do) does not require subjunctive. Para que + conjugated verb requires subjunctive. You can restructure sentences to use para + infinitive to avoid the subjunctive.
teachings.rule: Para + infinitive = no subjunctive needed. Para que + verb = subjunctive required.

examples.english: I called you again in order to organize

examples.spanish: Volví a llamarte para que organizáramos
examples.english: I called you again so that we organized

teachings.notes: You can sometimes restructure a sentence with para + infinitive to avoid the subjunctive.

teachings.summary: Puerto (port) and puerta (door) are related. Stress the English 'port', change vowel to get puerto; change -o to -a to get puerta.
teachings.notes: Port → puerto (stress the o) → puerta (change o to a).
